# debug

[![Build Status](https://travis-ci.org/debug-js/debug.svg?branch=master)](https://travis-ci.org/debug-js/debug)
[![Coverage Status](https://coveralls.io/repos/github/debug-js/debug/badge.svg?branch=master)](https://coveralls.io/github/debug-js/debug?branch=master)
[![npm version](https://badge.fury.io/js/debug.svg)](https://badge.fury.io/js/debug)
[![License: MIT](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-yellow.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

A tiny JavaScript debugging utility modelled after Node.js core's debugging technique. Works in Node.js and web browsers.

## Installation

```bash
$ npm install debug
```

## Usage

`debug` exposes a function; simply pass this function the name of your module, and it will return a decorated version of `console.error` for you to pass debug statements to. This will allow you to toggle the debug output for different parts of your module as well as the module as a whole.

Example **app.js**:

```js
const debug = require('debug')('app');
const http = require('http');
const name = 'My App';

// fake app

debug('booting %o', name);

http.createServer((req, res) => {
  debug(`${req.method} ${req.url}`);
  res.end('hello\n');
}).listen(3000, () => {
  debug('listening on port 3000');
});

// fake worker of some kind

require('./worker');
```

Example **worker.js**:

```js
const debug = require('debug')('worker');

setInterval(() => {
  debug('doing some work');
}, 1000);
```

 When running this program with `DEBUG=app node app.js`, you will see:

```
  app booting My App +0ms
  app listening on port 3000 +2ms
```

When running with `DEBUG=app,worker node app.js`, you will see:

```
  app booting My App +0ms
  app listening on port 3000 +2ms
  worker doing some work +0ms
  worker doing some work +1s
  worker doing some work +1s
  ...
```

When running with `DEBUG=app:* node app.js`, you will see:

```
  app booting My App +0ms
  app listening on port 3000 +2ms
```

## Namespace Colors

Each debug instance has a color generated for it based on its namespace name. This helps when visually scanning the output to identify which debug messages belong to which debug instance.

## Wildcards

The `*` character may be used as a wildcard. Suppose for example your library has debuggers named "connect:bodyParser", "connect:compress", "connect:session", etc. You can enable all of them by using:

```bash
DEBUG=connect:* node app.js
```

You can also exclude specific debuggers by prefixing them with a "-" character. For example, `DEBUG=*,-connect:* node app.js` would include all debuggers except those starting with "connect:".

## Environment Variables

### Node.js

When running through Node.js, you can set a few environment variables that will change the behavior of the debug logging:

| Name      | Purpose                                         |
|-----------|-------------------------------------------------|
| `DEBUG`   | Enables/disables specific debugging namespaces  |
| `DEBUG_HIDE_DATE` | Hide date from debug output (non-TTY)   |
| `DEBUG_COLORS`| Whether or not to use colors in the debug output |
| `DEBUG_DEPTH` | Object inspection depth                     |
| `DEBUG_SHOW_HIDDEN` | Shows hidden properties on inspected objects |


### Browser

When running in the browser, you can set `localStorage.debug` to enable debugging:

```js
localStorage.debug = 'app,worker:*'
```

Disable debugging with:

```js
localStorage.debug = ''
```

## Formatters

Debug uses [printf-style](https://wikipedia.org/wiki/Printf_format_string) formatting. Below are the officially supported formatters:

| Formatter | Representation |
|-----------|----------------|
| `%O`      | Pretty-print an Object on multiple lines |
| `%o`      | Pretty-print an Object all on a single line |
| `%s`      | String |
| `%d`      | Number (both integer and float) |
| `%j`      | JSON. Replaced with the string '[Circular]' if the argument contains circular references |
| `%%`      | Single percent sign ('%'). This does not consume an argument |

### Custom formatters

You can add custom formatters by extending the `debug.formatters` object. For example, to add support for rendering a Buffer as hex with `%h`:

```js
const createDebug = require('debug')
const debug = createDebug('test')

// Custom formatter for %h
createDebug.formatters.h = (v) => {
  return v.toString('hex')
}

// Use the custom formatter
const name = 'tjholowaychuk';
const buf = Buffer.from(name);
debug('Name: %h', buf);
// Prints: test Name: 746a686f6c6f77617963687566
```

## Browser Support

Debug works in the browser as well, currently persisted by `localStorage`. Consider the situation shown below where you have `worker:a` and `worker:b` as shown below:

```js
var a = debug('worker:a');
var b = debug('worker:b');

setInterval(function(){
  a('doing some work');
}, 1000);

setInterval(function(){
  b('doing some work');
}, 1200);
```

Enabling debug output for the browser can be done via the console as follows:

```js
debug.enable('worker:*')
```

Or in localStorage directly:

```js
localStorage.debug = 'worker:*'
```

## Output streams

By default `debug` will log to stderr, however this can be configured per-namespace by overriding the `log` method:

```js
const debug = require('debug');
const error = debug('app:error');

// By default stderr is used
error('goes to stderr!');

// Override the log function for this specific namespace
error.log = console.log.bind(console);
error('goes to stdout');
```

## Extend

You can simply extend debugger
```js
const debug = require('debug');
const extend = require('util')._extend;
const appDebug = extend(debug('app:debug'), {
  myCustomMethod() {
    console.log('doing something custom');
  }
});
appDebug('doing something standard');
appDebug.myCustomMethod();
```

## Set dynamically

You can also enable debug dynamically by calling the `enable()` method:

```js
let debug = require('debug');

console.log(1, debug.enabled('test'));

debug.enable('test');
console.log(2, debug.enabled('test'));

debug.disable();
console.log(3, debug.enabled('test'));
```

Print:
```
1 false
2 true
3 false
```

Usage:
```js
debug.enable('foo:*,-foo:bar');
debug.disable(); // disables all previously enabled namespaces
```

## Checking whether a debug target is enabled

After you've created a debug instance, you can determine whether or not it is enabled by checking the `enabled` property:

```js
const debug = require('debug')('http');

if (debug.enabled) {
  // expensive operation
}
```
